content.text: Claude Code acts as an MCP client, connecting to external MCP servers that expose tools, resources, and prompts. Every connected server's tools appear in the active session under the naming prefix
, making third-party capabilities — GitHub, Jira, databases, internal APIs — directly callable by Claude without any custom integration code.

MCP (Model Context Protocol) in Claude Code is the mechanism through which Claude Code spawns or connects to external processes and makes their functionality available as first-class tools in the session. Two transports are supported:
: Claude Code launches the server as a child process and communicates via stdin/stdout. The process's lifecycle is tied to the Claude Code session.
: Claude Code connects to an already-running HTTP server that speaks the MCP protocol over Server-Sent Events. The server lives independently of the Claude Code session.
(project or user scope) or via the
CLI flag. Claude Code reads the configuration at startup, establishes connections, discovers each server's tools via the MCP
method, and registers them all under the

attrs.alt: MCP in Claude Code architecture: stdio and HTTP/SSE servers, configuration scopes, tool registry
attrs.title: MCP in Claude Code Architecture

content.text: Without MCP, Claude Code can only operate on the local filesystem through its built-in tools. With MCP servers connected, it can create GitHub issues, query databases, update Jira tickets, call internal APIs, and perform any action the connected servers expose — all within a single session, all accessible to Claude as naturally as any built-in tool. The MCP standard means any tool built for the protocol works with Claude Code without additional adaptation.
MCP servers are declared under the
. Each key is the server name (used to prefix tool calls); the value is the connection config:

Connect via stdio and negotiate capabilities.
Executable to launch (e.g. node, python3, npx)
Arguments array — separate tokens, not a single shell string
Extra env vars for the child process; $VAR references expand from the shell
Working directory for the child process (optional)
For servers that run as long-lived HTTP services (remote or local), use the

Claude Code makes a long-lived SSE connection to the URL at startup. The server pushes tool results back over the SSE stream. HTTP/SSE is the right choice for shared team servers, remote APIs, or servers written in languages that are slow to start.

When multiple scopes declare the same server name, the project scope overrides the user scope, and the CLI flag overrides both. This lets you override a shared team server with a local test version without editing the shared config.

How MCP Tools Appear in Claude Code
Every tool from a connected MCP server is available to Claude under the name
. For example, a GitHub MCP server named
Claude can discover and call these tools naturally. You can also reference them explicitly in instructions:

# In CLAUDE.md or a prompt:
Use mcp__github__create_pull_request to open PRs after making changes.
Avoid using mcp__filesystem__write_file directly — use the Edit tool instead.
Claude Code may defer loading MCP tool schemas to reduce startup time. If a tool is listed but its full schema is not yet loaded, use the
built-in tool to load the schema on demand:

ToolSearch(query: "select:mcp__github__create_pull_request,mcp__github__list_issues")
# Returns full JSON Schema for the requested tools so they become callable
Real-World Example: GitHub and Jira Together
With this configuration, a Claude Code session can:
Do all of this automatically when given a task like: "implement the feature described in JIRA-1234, open a PR, and mark the ticket as In Review."
If Claude Code reports that an MCP server failed to connect, the most common causes are:
: the command or binary does not exist. Run the command directly in a terminal to see the error.
: the server exits immediately because a required API key is not set. Check that your shell has the variable:
: args must be an array of separate tokens. A single string like
will not be split by the shell — it must be

# Run the server manually and send it an MCP initialize request over stdin
echo '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"method":"initialize","params":{"protocolVersion":"2025-06-18","capabilities":{}}}' \
  | GITHUB_TOKEN=$GITHUB_TOKEN npx -y @github/mcp-server

# A healthy server prints a JSON response; a broken one exits with an error
If the server connects but tools do not appear in the session:
Ask Claude: "What MCP tools are available?" — Claude will list all tools it can see.
Check if the server declares the
capability in its initialize response — a server without this capability exposes no tools.
Verify the server name in mcpServers matches what you expect the prefix to be.
Claude Code applies the same permission system to MCP tools as to built-in tools. You can restrict which MCP tools Claude may call without user approval by adding them to the allow or deny lists in
Denied tools are not callable — Claude sees an error if it attempts to use them. Tools not in either list require explicit user approval on each call.
Command args as a single shell string
Server fails to launch; wrong executable path
Secret committed to repo, leaked in git history
Same server name in user and project scope
Project config silently overrides user config
Use distinct server names or document the override intentionally
npx fetches latest on each run; behavior changes silently

Beyond tools, MCP servers can expose two additional capability types that Claude Code can access:
Resources are named, URI-addressable pieces of content — files, database records, API responses — that a server makes readable. Claude Code can fetch them as context:

Claude can reference these URIs when building context for a task. For example: "Read the API spec from the MCP resources and implement the POST /users endpoint." The Claude Code session fetches the resource content via
Prompts are reusable message templates that a server exposes via
. They are less commonly used in Claude Code than tools, but useful when a server wants to provide canned instructions — for example, a code review prompt template with project-specific criteria:
flag lets CI pipelines inject a dynamic MCP config at invocation time without editing the repo's
. This is the right pattern for ephemeral credentials or environment-specific server URLs:
The generated config file is never committed — it is written to a temp path, injected once, and discarded after the job. Secrets stay in the CI secret store, not in the repository.
Scope servers to where they're needed
: internal database tools belong in the project's settings.json, not in the user-level config that affects every session on the machine.
: add destructive MCP tools (delete, purge, drop) to the deny list. Require explicit user approval for mutations beyond safe read-and-comment operations.
: if you register two GitHub servers (one for personal, one for org), give them different names (
Prefer official MCP SDKs for custom servers
: Anthropic and the MCP community maintain TypeScript and Python SDKs (
) that handle protocol framing, error responses, and capability negotiation correctly. Only write raw JSON-RPC when you need minimal dependencies.
Claude Code is an MCP client: it connects to MCP servers (stdio or HTTP/SSE) and registers their tools as mcp__<server>__<tool> in the session.
Configure servers in the mcpServers block of settings.json at user scope (~/.claude) or project scope (.claude). The CLI --mcp-config flag overrides both.
Never hardcode credentials in settings.json — use $ENV_VAR references and inject actual values via the shell or CI secrets.
Control access with the permissions allow/deny lists in settings.json. Dangerous tools (delete, create) should be denied or kept off the allow list to require explicit approval.
Debug by running the server command directly in a terminal with a test initialize payload over stdin to confirm it responds before wiring it into Claude Code.
